S01.title: Fairness metrics: choosing a definition and defending it
S01.subtitle: Three reasonable definitions. One dataset. Pick one — and learn why you cannot keep all three.

S04.title: Three definitions
S04.subtitle: Each captures something fairness intuitively requires. Each is a different statement about the model.

S12.title: The arithmetic
S12.subtitle: The impossibility is not something you take on authority. It becomes obvious once you see the numbers.

S19.title: The COMPAS case
S19.subtitle: Broward County, 2016. The theorem, in public, with real consequences.

S23.title: Beyond groups: individual fairness
S23.subtitle: A model can satisfy demographic parity while treating two nearly-identical people completely differently. Similar individuals should be treated similarly.

S27.title: Causal fairness
S27.subtitle: Statistical metrics read the data. But to tell discrimination from a justified disparity, you need a model of how the data was generated.

S31.title: The Generalized Entropy Index
S31.subtitle: Not “is it fair, yes/no” — but how much unfairness, and where it comes from. Borrowed from income-inequality economics.

S35.title: The defense is the deliverable
S35.subtitle: The toolkit lets you implement a choice. It does not make the choice for you — or absolve you of defending it.

S39.title: Understand the math. Choose the values. Defend the integration.
S39.subtitle: Three formally distinct metrics can be incompatible on one dataset when base rates differ. The impossibility is structural. The defense — connecting the chosen metric to the deployment, the cost-bearers, and the construct — is the work. Next — Chapter 8: adversarial robustness. Can a perturbation invisible to a human flip the model entirely?
